text: This video is to demonstrate robotic assisted radical parametrectomy with pelvic lymph node dissection in the case of CA cervix by Dr. Rohit Raghunath Ranade and his team. Here is a forty-six years old multiparous woman with previous two caesarean section. She had history of heavy menstrual bleeding, which was evaluated and treated elsewhere. The pre-operative ultrasound was suggestive of cervical fibroid, hence she had androgon, a total laparoscopic hysterectomy with bilateral salving oophorectomy one month back. Post-operative histopath was suggested for adenocarcinoma of cervix without ectocervical inflammation. Clinical examination was insignificant and the post-op CT showed no evidence of pelvic mass lesion or collection in the pelvis. No lymphadenopathy. Hence, she was planned for robotic assisted radical parametrectomy with pelvic lymphoma dissection.

The surgical procedure begins with port placement in the supraumbilical region using Hasen's method. The robotic system is then engaged. Post-operative adhesions between the vaginal vault and sigmoid colon are visible and carefully released using monopolar scissors. Additional adhesions between the sigmoid colon and left lateral pelvic wall are similarly addressed. The surgeon opens the right retroperitoneal space, identifying the iliac vessel and ureter, and proceeds to create the paravesical and pararectal spaces. The right uterine artery is visualized, traced to its origin, skeletonized, ligated with a ligar clip, cauterized, and cut. Developed pelvic spaces are visible.

The surgeon clamps the right infundibulopelvic ligament using a ligatril, then cauterizes and cuts it. The ureter is dissected further up to the ureteric tunnel. The medial pararectal space is created, and dissection proceeds downwards towards the rectum, followed by the creation of the pre-rectal space. The round ligament is cauterized and cut, and the anterior leaf of the broad ligament is dissected, continuing as a bladder dissection from the lateral aspect. The medial paravisical space and lateral parametrium are visualized.

The surgeon opens the left retroperitoneal space, identifying the ureter and iliac vessels. The uterine artery is traced to its origin, ligated with a clip, cauterized, and cut. The left infundibulopelvic ligament is similarly cauterized and cut after clipping. The peritoneal layer and infundibulopelvic ligament are dissected downwards, creating the left medial pararectal space and extending dissection to the prerectal space. The anterior leaf of the left broad ligament is dissected, and bladder dissection proceeds from the lateral aspect. The round ligament is cauterized and cut.

The surgeon identifies the lateral parametrium, also known as the paracervix, after developing the parava cycle and pararectal spaces. Bladder dissection is initiated from the lateral aspect using a Mecarni tube as a vaginal probe. The bladder peritoneum is identified and incised, and the bladder is dissected inferiorly up to the level of the middle third of the vagina.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the bladder pillar, joining the vesico-vaginal space to the paravesical space, effectively separating the bladder from the anterior vaginal wall. The ureters are mobilized and dissected down to the ureteric tunnel, isolated from the medial leaf of the peritoneum, and traced to their entry into the bladder. The lateral parametrium is excised after isolating the ureter, followed by an upper vaginectomy where the vagina is excised with an adequate margin. The same procedure is performed on the right side, using a liver shell through the assistance port to aid in dissection, and the specimen is excised.

The surgical specimen is delivered vaginally using an incarnate tube. The surgeon performs a radical pelvic lymph node dissection bilaterally, extending from the bifurcation of the common iliac artery cranially to the circumflex iliac vessel and the cupous ligament caudally. The dissection margins include the external iliac vessels laterally and the umbilical artery medially. Lymph nodes from the obturator space are removed and delivered vaginally.
The surgeon closes the vaginal vault using a 3-0 V-lock suture, ensuring hemostasis is achieved. The robotic system is then unlocked, and the port sites are closed.
